text:
Now I have a complete picture of the project. Let me plan the migration from Pages Router to App Router.

## Current Structure (Pages Router)
```
pages/
  index.js          - Home page with getServerSideProps (SSR)
  404.js             - Custom 404 page
  _app.js            - App wrapper (AppProvider, layout, global CSS)
  _document.js       - Custom Document (HTML, fonts, meta)
  _error.js          - Custom error page (getInitialProps - legacy API)
  api/
    posts/
      index.js       - GET/POST posts API
      [id].js        - GET/PUT/DELETE single post API
  blog/
    index.js         - Blog index with getStaticProps + ISR
    [id].js          - Blog post with getStaticPaths + getStaticProps + ISR
components/
  AppProvider.js     - React context provider
styles/
  globals.css        - Global styles
```

## App Router Structure (target)
```
app/
  layout.tsx         - Root layout (replaces _app.js + _document.js)
  page.tsx           - Home page (SSR with dynamic rendering)
  not-found.tsx      - 404 page (replaces 404.js)
  error.tsx          - Error page (replaces _error.js)
  globals.css        - Global styles (imported in layout)
  blog/
    page.tsx         - Blog index with ISR
    [id]/
      page.tsx       - Blog post with generateStaticParams + ISR
  api/
    posts/
      route.ts       - GET/POST posts API
      [id]/
        route.ts     - GET/PUT/DELETE single post API
```

## Key Migrations Needed:
1. **_app.js** → **app/layout.tsx** - Wrap with AppProvider, add header/footer layout, import global CSS
2. **_document.js** → **app/layout.tsx** metadata - Language, fonts, meta tags, favicon
3. **_error.js** → **app/error.tsx** - Error boundary with 'use client'
4. **404.js** → **app/not-found.tsx** - Custom not found page
5. **pages/index.js** → **app/page.tsx** - Home page with SSR (just make it a dynamic page by not generating)
6. **pages/blog/index.js** → **app/blog/page.tsx** - Blog index with ISR
7. **pages/blog/[id].js** → **app/blog/[id]/page.tsx** - Blog post with generateStaticParams + ISR
8. **pages/api/posts/index.js** → **app/api/posts/route.ts** - API routes using NextRequest/NextResponse
9. **pages/api/posts/[id].js** → **app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts** - API routes using NextRequest/NextResponse
10. **AppProvider.js** → Convert to .tsx with types
11. **styles/globals.css** → Move to app/globals.css

## API Conversions:
- `pages/api/*` uses `req, res` pattern → `app/api/*` uses `NextRequest` and exported functions like `GET()`, `POST()`, etc.
- `res.status(200).json(...)` → `NextResponse.json(...)`
- `req.query` → `params` from the route handler function signature
- `req.body` → `await req.json()`

## Page Conversions:
- `getServerSideProps` → For SSR, just make the page component `async` and fetch directly, or use dynamic rendering (no `generateStaticParams` / `revalidate`)
- `getStaticProps` → Fetch directly in the component and export `revalidate`
- `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams`
- `Head` from `next/head` → `metadata` export
- `useRouter` still works in App Router but with `next/navigation` instead of `next/router`
- `router.push` → `useRouter` from `next/navigation`
- `router.back` → `useRouter` from `next/navigation`
- `router.isFallback` → Not needed in App Router with `dynamicParams`

output: 1 --- 2 title: How to migrate from Pages to the App Router 3 nav_title: App Router 4 description: Learn how to upgrade your existing Next.js application from the Pages Router to the App Router. 5 --- 6 7 This guide will help you: 8 9 - [Update your Next.js application from version 12 to version 13](#nextjs-version) 10 - [Upgrade features that work in both the `pages` and the `app` directories](#upgrading-new-features) 11 - [Incrementally migrate your existing application from `pages` to `app`](#migrating-from-pages-to-app) 12 13 ## Upgrading 14 15 ### Node.js Version 16 17 The minimum Node.js version is now **v18.17**. See the [Node.js documentation](https://nodejs.org/docs/latest-v18.x/api/) for more information. 18 19 ### Next.js Version 20 21 To update to Next.js version 13, run the following command using your preferred package manager: 22 23 ```bash package="pnpm" 24 pnpm add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 25 ``` 26 27 ```bash package="npm" 28 npm install next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 29 ``` 30 31 ```bash package="yarn" 32 yarn add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 33 ``` 34 35 ```bash package="bun" 36 bun add next@latest react@latest react-dom@latest 37 ``` 38 39 ### ESLint Version 40 41 If you're using ESLint, you need to upgrade your ESLint version: 42 43 ```bash package="pnpm" 44 pnpm add -D eslint-config-next@latest 45 ``` 46 47 ```bash package="npm" 48 npm install -D eslint-config-next@latest 49 ``` 50 51 ```bash package="yarn" 52 yarn add -D eslint-config-next@latest 53 ``` 54 55 ```bash package="bun" 56 bun add -D eslint-config-next@latest 57 ``` 58 59 > **Good to know**: You may need to restart the ESLint server in VS Code for the ESLint changes to take effect. Open the Command Palette (`cmd+shift+p` on Mac; `ctrl+shift+p` on Windows) and search for `ESLint: Restart ESLint Server`. 60 61 ## Next Steps 62 63 After you've updated, see the following sections for next steps: 64 65 - [Upgrade new features](#upgrading-new-features): A guide to help you upgrade to new features such as the improved Image and Link Components. 66 - [Migrate from the `pages` to `app` directory](#migrating-from-pages-to-app): A step-by-step guide to help you incrementally migrate from the `pages` to the `app` directory. 67 68 ## Upgrading New Features 69 70 Next.js 13 introduced the new [App Router](/docs/app) with new features and conventions. The new Router is available in the `app` directory and co-exists with the `pages` directory. 71 72 Upgrading to Next.js 13 does **not** require using the App Router. You can continue using `pages` with new features that work in both directories, such as the updated [Image component](#image-component), [Link component](#link-component), [Script component](#script-component), and [Font optimization](#font-optimization). 73 74 ### `` Component 75 76 Next.js 12 introduced new improvements to the Image Component with a temporary import: `next/future/image`. These improvements included less client-side JavaScript, easier ways to extend and style images, better accessibility, and native browser lazy loading. 77 78 In version 13, this new behavior is now the default for `next/image`. 79 80 There are two codemods to help you migrate to the new Image Component: 81 82 - [**`next-image-to-legacy-image` codemod**](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#next-image-to-legacy-image): Safely and automatically renames `next/image` imports to `next/legacy/image`. Existing components will maintain the same behavior. 83 - [**`next-image-experimental` codemod**](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#next-image-experimental): Dangerously adds inline styles and removes unused props. This will change the behavior of existing components to match the new defaults. To use this codemod, you need to run the `next-image-to-legacy-image` codemod first. 84 85 ### `` Component 86 87 The [`` Component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) no longer requires manually adding an `` tag as a child. This behavior was added as an experimental option in [version 12.2](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12-2) and is now the default. In Next.js 13, `` always renders `` and allows you to forward props to the underlying tag. 88 89 For example: 90 91 ```jsx 92 import Link from 'next/link' 93 94 // Next.js 12: `` has to be nested otherwise it's excluded 95 96 About 97 98 99 // Next.js 13: `` always renders `` under the hood 100 101 About 102 103 ``` 104 105 To upgrade your links to Next.js 13, you can use the [`new-link` codemod](/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods#new-link). 106 107 ### `

1 --- 2 title: Layouts and Pages 3 description: Learn how to create your first pages and layouts, and link between them with the Link component. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout 10 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/page 11 - app/api-reference/components/link 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes 13 --- 14 15 Next.js uses **file-system based routing**, meaning you can use folders and files to define routes. This page will guide you through how to create layouts and pages, and link between them. 16 17 ## Creating a page 18 19 A **page** is UI that is rendered on a specific route. To create a page, add a [`page` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) inside the `app` directory and default export a React component. For example, to create an index page (`/`): 20 21 28 29 ```tsx filename="app/page.tsx" switcher 30 export default function Page() { 31 return

Hello Next.js!

32 } 33 ``` 34 35 ```jsx filename="app/page.js" switcher 36 export default function Page() { 37 return

Hello Next.js!

38 } 39 ``` 40 41 ## Creating a layout 42 43 A layout is UI that is **shared** between multiple pages. On navigation, layouts preserve state, remain interactive, and do not rerender. 44 45 You can define a layout by default exporting a React component from a [`layout` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout). The component should accept a `children` prop which can be a page or another [layout](#nesting-layouts). 46 47 For example, to create a layout that accepts your index page as child, add a `layout` file inside the `app` directory: 48 49 56 57 ```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher 58 export default function DashboardLayout({ 59 children, 60 }: { 61 children: React.ReactNode 62 }) { 63 return ( 64 65 66 {/* Layout UI */} 67 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 68
{children}
69 70 71 ) 72 } 73 ``` 74 75 ```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher 76 export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) { 77 return ( 78 79 80 {/* Layout UI */} 81 {/* Place children where you want to render a page or nested layout */} 82
{children}
83 84 85 ) 86 } 87 ``` 88 89 The layout above is called a [root layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#root-layout) because it's defined at the root of the `app` directory. The root layout is **required** and must contain `html` and `body` tags. 90 91 ## Creating a nested route 92 93 A nested route is a route composed of multiple URL segments. For example, the `/blog/[slug]` route is composed of three segments: 94 95 - `/` (Root Segment) 96 - `blog` (Segment) 97 - `[slug]` (Leaf Segment) 98 99 In Next.js: 100 101 - **Folders** are used to define the route segments that map to URL segments. 102 - **Files** (like `page` and `layout`) are used to create UI that is shown for a segment. 103 104 To create nested routes, you can nest folders inside each other. For example, to add a route for `/blog`, create a folder called `blog` in the `app` directory. Then, to make `/blog` publicly accessible, add a `page.tsx` file: 105 106 113 114 ```tsx filename="app/blog/page.tsx" switcher 115 // Dummy imports 116 import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 117 import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 118 119 export default async function Page() { 120 const posts = await getPosts() 121 122 return ( 123
    124 {posts.map((post) => ( 125 126 ))} 127
128 ) 129 } 130 ``` 131 132 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 133 // Dummy imports 134 import { getPosts } from '@/lib/posts' 135 import { Post } from '@/ui/post' 136 137 export default async function Page() { 138 const posts = await getPosts() 139 140 return ( 141
    142 {posts.map((post) => ( 143 144 ))} 145
146 ) 147 } 148 ``` 149 150 You can continue nesting folders to create nested routes. For example, to create a route for a specific blog post, create a new `[slug]` folder inside `blog` and add a `page` file: 151 152 159 160 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 161 function generateStaticParams() {} 162 163 export default function Page() { 164 return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

165 } 166 ``` 167 168 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 169 function generateStaticParams() {} 170 171 export default function Page() { 172 return

Hello, Blog Post Page!

173 } 174 ``` 175 176 Wrapping a folder name in square brackets (e.g. `[slug]`) creates a [dynamic route segment](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) which is used to generate multiple pages from data. e.g. blog posts, product pages, etc. 177 178 ## Nesting layouts 179 180 By default, layouts in the folder hierarchy are also nested, which means they wrap child layouts via their `children` prop. You can nest layouts by adding `layout` inside specific route segments (folders). 181 182 For example, to create a layout for the `/blog` route, add a new `layout` file inside the `blog` folder. 183 184 191 192 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 193 export default function BlogLayout({ 194 children, 195 }: { 196 children: React.ReactNode 197 }) { 198 return
{children}
199 } 200 ``` 201 202 ```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 203 export default function BlogLayout({ children }) { 204 return
{children}

For example, instead of manually creating a route for each individual blog post, you can create a dynamic segment to generate the routes based on blog post data. 213 214 To create a dynamic segment, wrap the segment (folder) name in square brackets: `[segmentName]`. For example, in the `app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx` route, the `[slug]` is the dynamic segment. 215 216 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 217 export default async function BlogPostPage({ 218 params, 219 }: { 220 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 221 }) { 222 const { slug } = await params 223 const post = await getPost(slug) 224 225 return ( 226

Use the `searchParams` prop when you need search parameters to **load data for the page** (e.g. pagination, filtering from a database). 281 - Use `useSearchParams` when search parameters are used **only on the client** (e.g. filtering a list already loaded via props). 282 - As a small optimization, you can use `new URLSearchParams(window.location.search)` in **callbacks or event handlers** to read search params without triggering re-renders. 283 284 ## Linking between pages 285 286 You can use the [`` component](/docs/app/api-reference/components/link) to navigate between routes. `` is a built-in Next.js component that extends the HTML `` tag to provide [prefetching](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching) and [client-side navigation](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#client-side-transitions). 287 288 For example, to generate a list of blog posts, import `` from `next/link` and pass a `href` prop to the component: 289 290 ```tsx filename="app/ui/post.tsx" highlight={1,10} switcher 291 import Link from 'next/link' 292 293 export default async function Post({ post }) { 294 const posts = await getPosts() 295 296 return ( 297
    298 {posts.map((post) => ( 299
  • 300 {post.title} 301
    • 302 ))} 303
304 ) 305 } 306 ``` 307 308 ```jsx filename="app/ui/post.js" highlight={1,10} switcher 309 import Link from 'next/link' 310 311 export default async function Post({ post }) { 312 const posts = await getPosts() 313 314 return ( 315
    316 {posts.map((post) => ( 317
  • 318 {post.title} 319
    • 320 ))} 321
322 ) 323 } 324 ``` 325 326 > **Good to know**: `` is the primary way to navigate between routes in Next.js. You can also use the [`useRouter` hook](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-router) for more advanced navigation. 327 328 ## Route Props Helpers 329 330 Next.js exposes utility types that infer `params` and named slots from your route structure: 331 332 - [**PageProps**](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page#page-props-helper): Props for `page` components, including `params` and `searchParams`. 333 - [**LayoutProps**](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout#layout-props-helper): Props for `layout` components, including `children` and any named slots (e.g. folders like `@analytics`). 334 335 These are globally available helpers, generated when running either `next dev`, `next build` or [`next typegen`](/docs/app/api-reference/cli/next#next-typegen-options). 336 337 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" 338 export default async function Page(props: PageProps<'/blog/[slug]'>) { 339 const { slug } = await props.params 340 return

Blog post: {slug}

341 } 342 ``` 343 344 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" 345 export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) { 346 return ( 347
348 {props.children} 349 {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */} 350 {/* {props.analytics} */} 351
352 ) 353 } 354 ``` 355 356 > **Good to know** 357 > 358 > - Static routes resolve `params` to `{}`. 359 > - `PageProps`, `LayoutProps` are global helpers — no imports required. 360 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 361

1 --- 2 title: Route Handlers 3 nav_title: Route Handlers 4 description: Learn how to use Route Handlers 5 related: 6 title: API Reference 7 description: Learn more about Route Handlers 8 links: 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/route 10 - app/guides/backend-for-frontend 11 --- 12 13 ## Route Handlers 14 15 Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs. 16 17 24 25 > **Good to know**: Route Handlers are only available inside the `app` directory. They are the equivalent of [API Routes](/docs/pages/building-your-application/routing/api-routes) inside the `pages` directory meaning you **do not** need to use API Routes and Route Handlers together. 26 27 ### Convention 28 29 Route Handlers are defined in a [`route.js|ts` file](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route) inside the `app` directory: 30 31 ```ts filename="app/api/route.ts" switcher 32 export async function GET(request: Request) {} 33 ``` 34 35 ```js filename="app/api/route.js" switcher 36 export async function GET(request) {} 37 ``` 38 39 Route Handlers can be nested anywhere inside the `app` directory, similar to `page.js` and `layout.js`. But there **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route segment level as `page.js`. 40 41 ### Supported HTTP Methods 42 43 The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`. If an unsupported method is called, Next.js will return a `405 Method Not Allowed` response. 44 45 ### Extended `NextRequest` and `NextResponse` APIs 46 47 In addition to supporting the native [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs, Next.js extends them with [`NextRequest`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) and [`NextResponse`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-response) to provide convenient helpers for advanced use cases. 48 49 ### Caching 50 51 Route Handlers are not cached by default. You can, however, opt into caching for `GET` methods. Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached. To cache a `GET` method, use a [route config option](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route-segment-config#dynamic) such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'` in your Route Handler file. 52 53 ```ts filename="app/items/route.ts" switcher 54 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 55 56 export async function GET() { 57 const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', { 58 headers: { 59 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 60 'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY, 61 }, 62 }) 63 const data = await res.json() 64 65 return Response.json({ data }) 66 } 67 ``` 68 69 ```js filename="app/items/route.js" switcher 70 export const dynamic = 'force-static' 71 72 export async function GET() { 73 const res = await fetch('https://data.mongodb-api.com/...', { 74 headers: { 75 'Content-Type': 'application/json', 76 'API-Key': process.env.DATA_API_KEY, 77 }, 78 }) 79 const data = await res.json() 80 81 return Response.json({ data }) 82 } 83 ``` 84 85 > **Good to know**: Other supported HTTP methods are **not** cached, even if they are placed alongside a `GET` method that is cached, in the same file. 86 87 #### With Cache Components 88 89 When [Cache Components](/docs/app/getting-started/cache-components) is enabled, `GET` Route Handlers follow the same model as normal UI routes in your application. They run at request time by default, can be prerendered when they don't access dynamic or runtime data, and you can use `use cache` to include dynamic data in the static response. 90 91 **Static example** - doesn't access dynamic or runtime data, so it will be prerendered at build time: 92 93 ```tsx filename="app/api/project-info/route.ts" 94 export async function GET() { 95 return Response.json({ 96 projectName: 'Next.js', 97 }) 98 } 99 ``` 100 101 **Dynamic example** - accesses non-deterministic operations. During the build, prerendering stops when `Math.random()` is called, deferring to request-time rendering: 102 103 ```tsx filename="app/api/random-number/route.ts" 104 export async function GET() { 105 return Response.json({ 106 randomNumber: Math.random(), 107 }) 108 } 109 ``` 110 111 **Runtime data example** - accesses request-specific data. Prerendering terminates when runtime APIs like `headers()` are called: 112 113 ```tsx filename="app/api/user-agent/route.ts" 114 import { headers } from 'next/headers' 115 116 export async function GET() { 117 const headersList = await headers() 118 const userAgent = headersList.get('user-agent') 119 120 return Response.json({ userAgent }) 121 } 122 ``` 123 124 > **Good to know**: Prerendering stops if the `GET` handler accesses network requests, database queries, async file system operations, request object properties (like `req.url`, `request.headers`, `request.cookies`, `request.body`), runtime APIs like [`cookies()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/cookies), [`headers()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/headers), [`connection()`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/connection), or non-deterministic operations. 125 126 **Cached example** - accesses dynamic data (database query) but caches it with `use cache`, allowing it to be included in the prerendered response: 127 128 ```tsx filename="app/api/products/route.ts" 129 import { cacheLife } from 'next/cache' 130 131 export async function GET() { 132 const products = await getProducts() 133 return Response.json(products) 134 } 135 136 async function getProducts() { 137 'use cache' 138 cacheLife('hours') 139 140 return await db.query('SELECT * FROM products') 141 } 142 ``` 143 144 > **Good to know**: `use cache` cannot be used directly inside a Route Handler body; extract it to a helper function. Cached responses revalidate according to `cacheLife` when a new request arrives. 145 146 ### Special Route Handlers 147 148 Special Route Handlers like [`sitemap.ts`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap), [`opengraph-image.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image), and [`icon.tsx`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons), and other [metadata files](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) remain static by default unless they use Dynamic APIs or dynamic config options. 149 150 ### Route Resolution 151 152 You can consider a `route` the lowest level routing primitive. 153 154 - They **do not** participate in layouts or client-side navigations like `page`. 155 - There **cannot** be a `route.js` file at the same route as `page.js`. 156 157 | Page | Route | Result | 158 | -------------------- | ------------------ | ---------------------------- | 159 | `app/page.js` | `app/route.js` | Conflict | 160 | `app/page.js` | `app/api/route.js` | Valid | 161 | `app/[user]/page.js` | `app/api/route.js` | Valid | 162 163 Each `route.js` or `page.js` file takes over all HTTP verbs for that route. 164 165 ```ts filename="app/page.ts" switcher 166 export default function Page() { 167 return

Hello, Next.js!

168 } 169 170 // Conflict 171 // `app/route.ts` 172 export async function POST(request: Request) {} 173 ``` 174 175 ```js filename="app/page.js" switcher 176 export default function Page() { 177 return

Hello, Next.js!

178 } 179 180 // Conflict 181 // `app/route.js` 182 export async function POST(request) {} 183 ``` 184 185 Read more about how Route Handlers [complement your frontend application](/docs/app/guides/backend-for-frontend), or explore the Route Handlers [API Reference](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route). 186 187 ### Route Context Helper 188 189 In TypeScript, you can type the `context` parameter for Route Handlers with the globally available [`RouteContext`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route#route-context-helper) helper: 190 191 ```ts filename="app/users/[id]/route.ts" switcher 192 import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server' 193 194 export async function GET(_req: NextRequest, ctx: RouteContext<'/users/[id]'>) { 195 const { id } = await ctx.params 196 return Response.json({ id }) 197 } 198 ``` 199 200 > **Good to know** 201 > 202 > - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`. 203

1 --- 2 title: Error Handling 3 description: Learn how to display expected errors and handle uncaught exceptions. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the features mentioned in this page by reading the API Reference. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/functions/redirect 9 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/error 10 - app/api-reference/functions/not-found 11 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/not-found 12 --- 13 14 Errors can be divided into two categories: [expected errors](#handling-expected-errors) and [uncaught exceptions](#handling-uncaught-exceptions). This page will walk you through how you can handle these errors in your Next.js application. 15 16 ## Handling expected errors 17 18 Expected errors are those that can occur during the normal operation of the application, such as those from [server-side form validation](/docs/app/guides/forms) or failed requests. These errors should be handled explicitly and returned to the client. 19 20 ### Server Functions 21 22 You can use the [`useActionState`](https://react.dev/reference/react/useActionState) hook to handle expected errors in [Server Functions](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-functions). 23 24 For these errors, avoid using `try`/`catch` blocks and throw errors. Instead, model expected errors as return values. 25 26 ```ts filename="app/actions.ts" switcher 27 'use server' 28 29 export async function createPost(prevState: any, formData: FormData) { 30 const title = formData.get('title') 31 const content = formData.get('content') 32 33 const res = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/posts', { 34 method: 'POST', 35 body: { title, content }, 36 }) 37 const json = await res.json() 38 39 if (!res.ok) { 40 return { message: 'Failed to create post' } 41 } 42 } 43 ``` 44 45 ```js filename="app/actions.js" switcher 46 'use server' 47 48 export async function createPost(prevState, formData) { 49 const title = formData.get('title') 50 const content = formData.get('content') 51 52 const res = await fetch('https://api.vercel.app/posts', { 53 method: 'POST', 54 body: { title, content }, 55 }) 56 const json = await res.json() 57 58 if (!res.ok) { 59 return { message: 'Failed to create post' } 60 } 61 } 62 ``` 63 64 You can pass your action to the `useActionState` hook and use the returned `state` to display an error message. 65 66 ```tsx filename="app/ui/form.tsx" highlight={11,19} switcher 67 'use client' 68 69 import { useActionState } from 'react' 70 import { createPost } from '@/app/actions' 71 72 const initialState = { 73 message: '', 74 } 75 76 export function Form() { 77 const [state, formAction, pending] = useActionState(createPost, initialState) 78 79 return ( 80
81 82 83 84

1 --- 2 title: Metadata and OG images 3 description: Learn how to add metadata to your pages and create dynamic OG images. 4 related: 5 title: API Reference 6 description: Learn more about the Metadata APIs mentioned in this page. 7 links: 8 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata 9 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-viewport 10 - app/api-reference/functions/image-response 11 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata 12 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons 13 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image 14 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots 15 - app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap 16 - app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots 17 --- 18 19 The Metadata APIs can be used to define your application metadata for improved SEO and web shareability and include: 20 21 1. [The static `metadata` object](#static-metadata) 22 2. [The dynamic `generateMetadata` function](#generated-metadata) 23 3. Special [file conventions](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata) that can be used to add static or dynamically generated [favicons](#favicons) and [OG images](#static-open-graph-images). 24 25 With all the options above, Next.js will automatically generate the relevant `` tags for your page, which can be inspected in the browser's developer tools. 26 27 The `metadata` object and `generateMetadata` function exports are only supported in Server Components. 28 29 ## Default fields 30 31 There are two default `meta` tags that are always added even if a route doesn't define metadata: 32 33 - The [meta charset tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Element/meta#attr-charset) sets the character encoding for the website. 34 - The [meta viewport tag](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTML/Viewport_meta_tag) sets the viewport width and scale for the website to adjust for different devices. 35 36 ```html 37 38 39 ``` 40 41 The other metadata fields can be defined with the `Metadata` object (for [static metadata](#static-metadata)) or the `generateMetadata` function (for [generated metadata](#generated-metadata)). 42 43 ## Static metadata 44 45 To define static metadata, export a [`Metadata` object](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-object) from a static [`layout.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout) or [`page.js`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) file. For example, to add a title and description to the blog route: 46 47 ```tsx filename="app/blog/layout.tsx" switcher 48 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 49 50 export const metadata: Metadata = { 51 title: 'My Blog', 52 description: '...', 53 } 54 55 export default function Layout() {} 56 ``` 57 58 ```jsx filename="app/blog/layout.js" switcher 59 export const metadata = { 60 title: 'My Blog', 61 description: '...', 62 } 63 64 export default function Layout() {} 65 ``` 66 67 You can view a full list of available options, in the [`generateMetadata` documentation](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#metadata-fields). 68 69 ## Generated metadata 70 71 You can use [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) function to `fetch` metadata that depends on data. For example, to fetch the title and description for a specific blog post: 72 73 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 74 import type { Metadata, ResolvingMetadata } from 'next' 75 76 type Props = { 77 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 78 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 79 } 80 81 export async function generateMetadata( 82 { params, searchParams }: Props, 83 parent: ResolvingMetadata 84 ): Promise { 85 const slug = (await params).slug 86 87 // fetch post information 88 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 89 res.json() 90 ) 91 92 return { 93 title: post.title, 94 description: post.description, 95 } 96 } 97 98 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }: Props) {} 99 ``` 100 101 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 102 export async function generateMetadata({ params, searchParams }, parent) { 103 const slug = (await params).slug 104 105 // fetch post information 106 const post = await fetch(`https://api.vercel.app/blog/${slug}`).then((res) => 107 res.json() 108 ) 109 110 return { 111 title: post.title, 112 description: post.description, 113 } 114 } 115 116 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) {} 117 ``` 118 119 ### Streaming metadata 120 121 For dynamically rendered pages, Next.js streams metadata separately, injecting it into the HTML once `generateMetadata` resolves, without blocking UI rendering. 122 123 Streaming metadata improves perceived performance by allowing visual content to stream first. 124 125 Streaming metadata is **disabled for bots and crawlers** that expect metadata to be in the `` tag (e.g. `Twitterbot`, `Slackbot`, `Bingbot`). These are detected by using the User Agent header from the incoming request. 126 127 You can customize or **disable** streaming metadata completely, with the [`htmlLimitedBots`](/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/htmlLimitedBots#disabling) option in your Next.js config file. 128 129 Statically rendered pages don’t use streaming since metadata is resolved at build time. 130 131 Learn more about [streaming metadata](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#streaming-metadata). 132 133 ### Memoizing data requests 134 135 There may be cases where you need to fetch the **same** data for metadata and the page itself. To avoid duplicate requests, you can use React's [`cache` function](https://react.dev/reference/react/cache) to memoize the return value and only fetch the data once. For example, to fetch the blog post information for both the metadata and the page: 136 137 ```ts filename="app/lib/data.ts" highlight={5} switcher 138 import { cache } from 'react' 139 import { db } from '@/app/lib/db' 140 141 // getPost will be used twice, but execute only once 142 export const getPost = cache(async (slug: string) => { 143 const res = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ where: eq(posts.slug, slug) }) 144 return res 145 }) 146 ``` 147 148 ```js filename="app/lib/data.js" highlight={5} switcher 149 import { cache } from 'react' 150 import { db } from '@/app/lib/db' 151 152 // getPost will be used twice, but execute only once 153 export const getPost = cache(async (slug) => { 154 const res = await db.query.posts.findFirst({ where: eq(posts.slug, slug) }) 155 return res 156 }) 157 ``` 158 159 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 160 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 161 162 export async function generateMetadata({ 163 params, 164 }: { 165 params: { slug: string } 166 }) { 167 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 168 return { 169 title: post.title, 170 description: post.description, 171 } 172 } 173 174 export default async function Page({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { 175 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 176 return
{post.title}
177 } 178 ``` 179 180 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 181 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 182 183 export async function generateMetadata({ params }) { 184 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 185 return { 186 title: post.title, 187 description: post.description, 188 } 189 } 190 191 export default async function Page({ params }) { 192 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 193 return
{post.title}
194 } 195 ``` 196 197 ## File-based metadata 198 199 The following special files are available for metadata: 200 201 - [favicon.ico, apple-icon.jpg, and icon.jpg](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons) 202 - [opengraph-image.jpg and twitter-image.jpg](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image) 203 - [robots.txt](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/robots) 204 - [sitemap.xml](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/sitemap) 205 206 You can use these for static metadata, or you can programmatically generate these files with code. 207 208 ## Favicons 209 210 Favicons are small icons that represent your site in bookmarks and search results. To add a favicon to your application, create a `favicon.ico` and add to the root of the app folder. 211 212 219 220 > You can also programmatically generate favicons using code. See the [favicon docs](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/app-icons) for more information. 221 222 ## Static Open Graph images 223 224 Open Graph (OG) images are images that represent your site in social media. To add a static OG image to your application, create a `opengraph-image.jpg` file in the root of the app folder. 225 226 233 234 You can also add OG images for specific routes by creating a `opengraph-image.jpg` deeper down the folder structure. For example, to create an OG image specific to the `/blog` route, add a `opengraph-image.jpg` file inside the `blog` folder. 235 236 243 244 The more specific image will take precedence over any OG images above it in the folder structure. 245 246 > Other image formats such as `jpeg`, `png`, and `gif` are also supported. See the [Open Graph Image docs](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/metadata/opengraph-image) for more information. 247 248 ## Generated Open Graph images 249 250 The [`ImageResponse` constructor](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/image-response) allows you to generate dynamic images using JSX and CSS. This is useful for OG images that depend on data. 251 252 For example, to generate a unique OG image for each blog post, add a `opengraph-image.tsx` file inside the `blog` folder, and import the `ImageResponse` constructor from `next/og`: 253 254 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/opengraph-image.tsx" switcher 255 import { ImageResponse } from 'next/og' 256 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 257 258 // Image metadata 259 export const size = { 260 width: 1200, 261 height: 630, 262 } 263 264 export const contentType = 'image/png' 265 266 // Image generation 267 export default async function Image({ params }: { params: { slug: string } }) { 268 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 269 270 return new ImageResponse( 271 ( 272 // ImageResponse JSX element 273
284 {post.title} 285
286 ) 287 ) 288 } 289 ``` 290 291 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/opengraph-image.js" switcher 292 import { ImageResponse } from 'next/og' 293 import { getPost } from '@/app/lib/data' 294 295 // Image metadata 296 export const size = { 297 width: 1200, 298 height: 630, 299 } 300 301 export const contentType = 'image/png' 302 303 // Image generation 304 export default async function Image({ params }) { 305 const post = await getPost(params.slug) 306 307 return new ImageResponse( 308 ( 309 // ImageResponse JSX element 310
321 {post.title} 322
323 ) 324 ) 325 } 326 ``` 327 328 `ImageResponse` supports common CSS properties including flexbox and absolute positioning, custom fonts, text wrapping, centering, and nested images. [See the full list of supported CSS properties](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/image-response). 329 330 > **Good to know**: 331 > 332 > - Examples are available in the [Vercel OG Playground](https://og-playground.vercel.app/). 333 > - `ImageResponse` uses [`@vercel/og`](https://vercel.com/docs/og-image-generation), [`satori`](https://github.com/vercel/satori), and `resvg` to convert HTML and CSS into PNG. 334 > - Only flexbox and a subset of CSS properties are supported. Advanced layouts (e.g. `display: grid`) will not work. 335

All tasks completed. Here's a summary of the full migration:

## Migration Summary: Pages Router → App Router

### What was migrated

| Pages Router | App Router | Key Changes |
|---|---|---|
| `pages/_app.js` + `pages/_document.js` | `app/layout.tsx` | Root layout with `metadata` export, `next/font/google` for Inter (replaces `<link>` tags), inline header/footer |
| `pages/index.js` | `app/page.tsx` | `getServerSideProps` → `headers()` from `next/headers` + async server component. `<a>` → `<Link>`, `next/head` → server component markup |
| `pages/404.js` | `app/not-found.tsx` | `useRouter` → `<Link>` (server component, no client JS needed) |
| `pages/_error.js` | `app/error.tsx` | `getInitialProps` (legacy) → `'use client'` error boundary with `error`/`reset` props |
| `pages/blog/index.js` | `app/blog/page.tsx` | `getStaticProps` → async component with `revalidate = 60`. `next/head` → `metadata` export |
| `pages/blog/[id].js` | `app/blog/[id]/page.tsx` | `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams()`. `getStaticProps` → async component + `revalidate = 300`. `router.isFallback` removed (blocking default in App Router). `router.back()` → `<Link href="/blog">` |
| `pages/api/posts/index.js` | `app/api/posts/route.ts` | `req/res` → `GET()`/`POST()` with `NextRequest`/`Response.json()`. 405 auto-handled |
| `pages/api/posts/[id].js` | `app/api/posts/[id]/route.ts` | `req/res` → `GET()`/`PUT()`/`DELETE()`. `req.query` → `context.params.id` (Promise) |
| `components/AppProvider.js` | `components/AppProvider.tsx` | Converted to TypeScript with `AppContextType` interface |
| `styles/globals.css` | `app/globals.css` | Moved to `app/` convention, imported by root layout |

### Deleted
- `pages/` directory entirely
- `styles/` directory (moved into `app/`)
- `components/AppProvider.js` (replaced by `.tsx`)

### APIs replaced
- `next/head` → `metadata`/`generateMetadata` exports + inline `<title>` in server components
- `next/router` (`useRouter`) → `next/navigation` (`useRouter` for client, `<Link>` for server)
- `getServerSideProps` → `headers()` + async server component (dynamic rendering)
- `getStaticProps` → async server component + `revalidate` export
- `getStaticPaths` → `generateStaticParams`
- Pages API routes (`req, res`) → Route handlers (`GET/POST/PUT/DELETE` functions with `NextRequest`/`Response`)
